name: Tammuz: Blood & Sand
short_description: A mythic VR puzzle-box adventure where ancient mechanisms come alive beneath your hands. Solve over 20 unique trials inside a divine cube prison and uncover Mesopotamian secrets that blur the line between reality and legend.
detailed_description:
Tammuz: Blood and Sand is a mythic VR puzzle-box adventure inspired by ancient Mesopotamian legends. Trapped inside a mysterious cube that serves as both prison and enigma, you’ll solve more than 20 intricate physical puzzles to uncover the secrets of forgotten gods. Every mechanism, every echo, and every illusion brings you closer to the truth buried in the sands of time.

Step into the mind of an imprisoned deity and experience a seated XR puzzle journey that challenges your logic, perception, and resolve. Between the whispers of eternity and the grinding of divine gears, your every move carries meaning.

Ancient prison. Divine guardian. Enter the cycle of Blood and Sand.

  • Innovative Virtual-Reality Puzzles
    Harness VR technology to blur the line between reality and the divine, crafting a grounded yet magical experience.

  • Tactile Interactions
    Every mechanism behaves as it would in reality — slide, twist, press, and align. Your hands become the key.

  • A Story Etched in Sand and Memory
    Unravel a psychological tale of power, sacrifice, and rebirth told through the fractured voice of the gods themselve

  • Atmospheric Worldbuilding
    Explore the convergence of ancient myth and futuristic design through immersive visuals and haunting soundscapes.

Join our Discord Community to share feedback, exchange theories, and help forge the next evolution of XR puzzle adventures.

Will you outwit an eternal guardian and set a god free, or will the echoes of the past claim you forever?

reviews.review_text:
[h1]Experienced on the Meta Quest 3[/h1]

You can view my gameplay & early impressions here: https://youtu.be/NptE-vnLLT8

I do think this is a solid VR puzzle game. Beware though: this isn't like Ghost Town or Red Matter. This is a puzzle game focused mostly on the puzzles. Yes, there are narrative cut scenes. So technically it is a puzzle adventure game. However, the focus on the game is on the puzzles and less so on the narrative. So you will be doing puzzle after puzzle, with only some respite in between the few cut scenes.

The puzzles, at least early on, are not too difficult. However, they're definitely more challenging than the puzzles found on Red Matter or Ghost Town, at least in my opinion. The challenge slowly picks up as you try to solve the box puzzles. Yes, if you played The Room (old pancake version), you will know exactly the type of puzzle game this is. You will need to observe the box and figure out what you need to do. There will be a lot of tinkering just to try to figure out the logic. 

Game is running on the Unity Engine. On my RTX 5090, I was getting a steady 90 fps. You do have full locomotion, but there is no artificial turning. However, it doesn't matter because you can turn the puzzles around. You can play comfortably seated. I did find that some of the interactions involving the puzzles could have used more polish.

[b]Rate ?/10.[/b]

[quote]Please note: I received a free Steam key through the Steam Curator Connect program. If you enjoyed my review, please consider joining my Steam Curator group [url=https://steamcommunity.com/groups/Oculus_WMR]Oculus Rift Reviews.[/url][/quote]

A great puzzle game following the style of classic "ROOM" games, focusing on one "object". 

As a more or less experienced puzzle gamer you might take about 4 hours to beat the game.
The puzzles themselves are never too hard and pretty fair - though I still have my suspicions there is some cheating going on in the final "puzzle" (just kidding).

I had much fun solving the puzzles, hope we get more content, a DLC or a sequel some day.

Stand in front of pedestal and solve puzzles for a 2D god. 

The puzzles range from pretty straight forward to I don't know what the mechanic is so I don't know how to solve it. I had to stop the last puzzle I was working on because I had to look down, even when the pedestal could be moved up and down, which with a VR headset on caused me to start getting sore in my neck from looking down. It is a fun experience to be in VR but it is also a curse. The controls could be a bit finicky at times but for the most part were fine. 

I recommend this if you love puzzles and want to be in VR.

As someone who loves puzzle games, Tammuz: Blood and Sand had my brain gears turning from start to finish. The puzzles aren’t obvious, but with some careful thought, they’re all completely solvable. The story is intriguing and kept me engaged the whole way through.

What really caught me off guard was my favorite part of the game, and it’s not even a puzzle. It’s the inclusion of an ancient tabletop board game called Ur, nearly 5,000 years old. After a few rounds, I started to understand how to strategically outsmart Tammuz, and that was such a clever and fun touch.

The controls and mechanics can feel a little fidgety at times, but if you’re patient, it’s easy enough to work through. Be sure to watch through the credits for a surprise. 

If you find yourself struggling on a puzzle, check out my walkthrough video on YouTube @misschiefvr.
